text: The surgeon begins by taking down the omentum, which appears thickened due to previous inflammation, revealing a gallbladder that is not actively inflamed.
The surgeon carefully removes surrounding tissue from the gallbladder to clearly expose the gallbladder wall, neck, and infundibulum.
The surgeon applies upper traction with assistance, achieving self-retaining retraction on the gallbladder area, while bringing the omentum and duodenum downwards.
The surgeon uses hook cautery near the duodenum, with visible thermal spread affecting adjacent tissues. Cold scissors are introduced for safer dissection.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to carefully dissect tissue, minimizing thermal spread near the duodenum.

The surgeon has mobilized the gallbladder, revealing its bottom. They proceed to open the peritoneum on the right side of the gallbladder, enhancing retraction and manipulation to achieve the critical view.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect up the left side of the gallbladder, ensuring careful identification of the artery in the vicinity.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum and surrounding tissue to avoid accidentally hooking the arteries. The artery is visualized and gently lateralized, moving it away from the gallbladder wall towards the Calot's triangle.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum directly over the gallbladder, heading towards the liver, and proceeds to dissect up the left side, mirroring the previous dissection on the right side by taking the peritoneum.
The surgeon further mobilizes the artery, carefully separating it from the cystic duct to prevent any aberrant vessels or misidentification of the hepatic artery.
The surgeon traces the artery up onto the gallbladder, ensuring no aberrant vasculature is present, while mobilizing it to drape back towards the Calot's triangle to achieve the critical view.
The surgeon switches between ICG mode and standard view to better identify the common duct and confirm the presence of a long cystic duct without branching.
The surgeon cleans off the cystic duct to achieve the critical view, utilizing lateral mobilization on the right side of the gallbladder to assist in clearing the posterior aspect of the duct.
The surgeon pauses before taking thicker structures, using ICG imaging to confirm the absence of aberrant ductal structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the achieved critical view of safety, clearly exposing the cystic duct and artery. The surgeon then proceeds to clip the artery followed by the duct.
The surgeon divides the cystic duct and artery, then proceeds to stay in the adventitial plane to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed.
The surgeon retrieves the gallbladder from the surgical field, taking a final look at the anatomy. The bifurcation of the hepatic ducts is clearly visible.
